\section*{Pack 1 (1 $\implies$ 2)}
Assume $G$ is connected and acyclic.  The graph must have a leaf. Removing a leaf and its adjacent edges reduces the number of edges to exactly $m-1$. This proves (2).

\section*{Proof: (2) $\implies$ (3)}
Assume $G$ is connected and has $m \leq n-1$ edges.  The goal is to show that $G$ is acyclic. Suppose, for the sake of contradiction, that $G$ contains a cycle. Removing an edge from this cycle results in a graph with fewer edges than $n-1$, which contradicts our assumption.

\section*{Characterization of Trees and Spanning Trees}
Today: Characterization of Trees and Spanning Trees. 

\textbf{Theorem:} Let $G$ be a finite graph with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges. The following are equivalent:
    (1) $G$ is connected and acyclic.
    (2) $G$ is connected and has no cycles.
    (3) $G$ is acyclic, and has no cycles.

\textbf{Proof:} (1 $\implies$ 2): Assume $G$ is connected and acyclic.  If $G$ had two largest paths, then it would contain a cycle. Removing these paths reduces the number of edges to exactly $n-1$.

\section*{Continuous breaking cycles (One at a time) until we get a connected acyclic graph G*}
Suppose no remaining initial $k \geq 1$ edges remain.  The resulting graph $G'$ is a tree! Some $G'$ is a tree and $|V(G')| = |V(G)| = n$.

\textbf{Theorem:} Let $G$ be a finite graph with $n$ vertices. If $G$ is connected and acyclic, then it is a tree.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item If $G$ is connected and acyclic
    \item $G$ is connected and monotonic
    \item $G$ is acyclic
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Proof:} (2 $\implies$ 3): Assume $G$ is connected and acyclic.  Removing an edge from a cycle eliminates the cycle, resulting in a graph with no cycles.

\section*{Continuous Breaking Cycles (Once at a time) until we get a connected and acyclic graph}
Suppose we have $k$ nodes. $k > 2$. Let $G$ be a connected and acyclic graph.  The resulting graph $G'$ is a tree! Some $G'$ is a tree and $|V(G')| = |V(G)| = n$. So $G$ has exactly $n-1-k$ edges.

\textbf{Exam Tip:} Understand the equivalence of conditions for a graph to be a tree, and be able to prove these equivalences.
